docs.title: External Dependencies
docs.text:
These are the software packages iProc was developed with and validated against.

Can I upgrade these dependencies?

iProc may run perfectly fine with newer versions of the dependencies shown below, but the results have never been validated.

Software Versions Download Python 3.11 GNU Parallel 20180522 AFNI 16.3.13 FSL 4.0.3, 5.0.4, 5.0.10, 6.0.1 MRIcron 2012_12_12 MRIcroGL 2019_09_04 FreeSurfer 6.0.0 ImageMagick 6.7.8-10 ANTs 2.4.4 dcm2niix 1.0.20230411

docs.title: FSL, FSL, Everywhere
docs.text:
Important

Until there is a portable solution in place (e.g., containers), end users need to modify the following lines of code to load the correct version of FSL at the appropriate time.

iProc dynamically switches FSL versions at runtime using Lmod. Your computing environment may not use Lmod, or your module names may differ. You need to understand where and how iProc is switching FSL versions and make the necessary modifications to the iProc source code to suit your environment.

docs.title: iProc.py
docs.text: Before running iProc.py, it is assumed that you have already loaded FSL v5.0.10.

docs.title: fmap_from_bids
docs.text:
Within runscript/fmap_from_bids.py, the FSL version is switched to v4.0.3 before running the FSL bet2 command.

cmd = f'module load fsl/4.0.3-ncf && {_cmd}'

docs.title: fmap_topup_prep
docs.text:
Within runscript/fmap_topup_prep.sh, the FSL version is switched to v6.0.1 before running the FSL topup command

module load fsl/6.0.1-ncf

docs.title: fm_unwarp_and_mc_to_midvol
docs.text:
In runscript/fm_unwarp_and_mc_to_midvol.sh, the FSL version is switched to v4.0.3 before running runscript/fm_uw.sh

${CODEDIR}/modwrap.sh 'module load fsl/4.0.3-ncf' 'module load fsl/5.0.4-ncf' [...]

docs.title: fmap_fsl_prepare_fieldmap_prep
docs.text:
In runscript/fmap_fsl_prepare_fieldmap_prep.sh, the FSL version is switched to v5.0.4 before running the FSL fsl_prepare_fieldmap command

module load fsl/5.0.4-ncf

docs.title: Welcome to the iProc Quickstart
docs.text:
Before you can proceed with your next mind-bending scientific discovery, you need to confirm that you have a fully functioning iProc installation. This guide will help you with that.

Looking for in depth documentation?

The intent of this quickstart is to ensure that you have iProc installed successfully. For more detailed iProc documentation, please refer to the Full Tutorial.

docs.title: clone the repository
docs.text:
First, you need to download iProc by cloning the repository from GitHub

git clone -b v2.6.0-beta.3 https://github.com/harvard-nrg/iProc

docs.title: create a virtual environment
docs.text:
iProc is primarily written in Python (and some bash scripts). You will need to create a Python virtual environment before you can move forward.

Python version

One of the iProc dependencies is numpy == 1.25.2, which is compatible with Python versions up to Python 3.11. If you want to use a newer version of Python, you will need to change pyproject.toml to install numpy == 1.26.2 or greater. Be advised that upgrading dependencies has not been validated.

cd iProc
python3.11 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install --upgrade pip

docs.title: install iProc
docs.text:
To install iProc and its Python dependencies, run the following command within your activated virtual environment

pip install .

docs.title: external dependencies
docs.text:
iProc requires several external software packages described on the following page.

FSL

iProc switches FSL versions at runtime. It is strongly advised that you read the following page to understand where and how this is done. You will almost certainly need to make modifications to iProc before it will run successfully within your environment.

docs.title: verifying your installation
docs.text:
Run the following command to verify that you are able to launch iProc

./iProc.py --help

If you're here, you're well on your way to getting iProc up and running. Way to go

docs.title: Example dataset
docs.text:
The following steps are highly choreographed and assume that you are running each command within your iProc base directory.

We have created an example dataset for you to verfiy that you have a fully functioning version of iProc installed.

docs.title: download and extract the dataset
docs.text:
Download the data set

Alternatively, you can copy the link to the dataset above and download the tarball using curl

curl -L 'paste link here' --output ASDF.tar.gz
tar xzf ASDF.tar.gz

FreeSurfer dummy data

The example data set includes precomputed FreeSurfer results under tutorial_data/fs. If this data is present, iProc will skip running FreeSurfer, which can save several hours of runtime. Remove the fs directory if you want iProc to run FreeSurfer.

docs.title: Running iProc
docs.text: Now for the moment you've been waiting for — let's run iProc. You got this

docs.title: set the base directory
docs.text:
Replace the {{BASEDIR}} placeholder within ASDF.cfg (line 3) with the location to your tutorial_data directory

DIR=$(pwd)/tutorial_data
sed -ir s@{{BASEDIR}}@${DIR}@g tutorial_data/mri_data_AP/ASDF/subject_lists/ASDF.cfg

docs.title: set the fsaverage6 directory
docs.text:
Replace the {{FSDIR}} placeholder within ASDF.cfg (last line) with the location to the fsaverage6 directory under your FreeSurfer installation

DIR=${FREESURFER_HOME}/subjects/fsaverage6
sed -ir s@{{FSDIR}}@${DIR}@g tutorial_data/mri_data_AP/ASDF/subject_lists/ASDF.cfg

docs.title: run iProc
docs.text:
Now, run the following command to launch the first iProc stage, setup

./iProc.py --config tutorial_data/mri_data_AP/subject_lists/ASDF.cfg --executor local --stage setup --bids $(pwd)/bids/sub-ASDF

After each stage of the pipeline, iProc will print instructions to the screen that will guide you to the next stage. The stages are setup, bet, unwarp_motioncorrect_align, T1_warp_and_mask, combine_and_apply_warp, and filter_and_project.
